reviews.summary: This work discusses an interesting security issue of deploying a model fine-tuned on a large foundational model in private downstream applications. It proposes a universal meta-initialized and gradient robust adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) to break the powerful SAM and its various downstream models, without requiring prior knowledge of the specific downstream task and data distribution. The author explores the challenges associated with threating transfer-based adversarial attack without the task-related prior knowledge and provides the theoretical insights on the deviation in updating the adversarial perturbation when using the open-sourced model as the surrogate model. An extensive evaluation of UMI-GRAT's performance, transferability, and efficiency was conducted across five datasets and three different downstream tasks (medical image segmentation, shadow segmentation and camouflaged object segmentation), demonstrating the high effectiveness of the UMI-GRAT approach.
reviews.strengths:
1. This work discusses a critical adversarial issue of deploying large foundation model in real-world applications and for the first time considers a more challenging and practical scenario where the adversarial attacker breaks SAM and its downstream models in the absence of prior knowledge on the task and data distribution.
2. This work provides the in-depth analysis on the challenge of threating the transferable adversarial attack via open-sourced SAM and proposes the corresponding theoretical insights and solution.  
3. The work establishes a detailed experimental framework and the proposed UMI-GRAT shows superior performance on misleading various SAMs’ downstream models compared with previous methods, which serve as a preliminary exploration for future research.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.	It’s recommended to give more comprehensive analysis of the UMI noise, including the size of the natural image dataset and the effect of various hyperparameters.
2.	There are more metrics such as $E_\phi$, $F_\beta^\omega$ in the camouflaged object detection task. It would be beneficial if the author could provide further data pertaining to these evaluation metrics to enrich the analysis.
reviews.questions:
1.	In Figure 3, the peak cosine similarity of the generated adversarial perturbation is observed between the 20th and 30th iteration. So will increasing the iterative step of generating adversarial perturbation enhance the transferability? 
2.	Model ensemble is an effective method to enhance the adversarial attacks’ transferability. Will the ensemble of different SAMs benefit the UMI-GRAT?
reviews.limitations: The experimental results are all based on SAMs and their downstream models. It will provide more valuable insights if expanding the scope of analysis to assess whether this adversarial threat also applies to other large foundation models.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work discusses an interesting security issue of deploying a model fine-tuned on a large foundational model in private downstream applications. It proposes a universal meta-initialized and gradient robust adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) to break the powerful SAM and its various downstream models, without requiring prior knowledge of the specific downstream task and data distribution. The author explores the challenges associated with threating transfer-based adversarial attack without the task-related prior knowledge and provides the theoretical insights on the deviation in updating the adversarial perturbation when using the open-sourced model as the surrogate model. An extensive evaluation of UMI-GRAT's performance, transferability, and efficiency was conducted across five datasets and three different downstream tasks (medical image segmentation, shadow segmentation and camouflaged object segmentation), demonstrating the high effectiveness of the UMI-GRAT approach.

Strengths:
1. This work discusses a critical adversarial issue of deploying large foundation model in real-world applications and for the first time considers a more challenging and practical scenario where the adversarial attacker breaks SAM and its downstream models in the absence of prior knowledge on the task and data distribution.
2. This work provides the in-depth analysis on the challenge of threating the transferable adversarial attack via open-sourced SAM and proposes the corresponding theoretical insights and solution.  
3. The work establishes a detailed experimental framework and the proposed UMI-GRAT shows superior performance on misleading various SAMs’ downstream models compared with previous methods, which serve as a preliminary exploration for future research.

Weaknesses:
1.	It’s recommended to give more comprehensive analysis of the UMI noise, including the size of the natural image dataset and the effect of various hyperparameters.
2.	There are more metrics such as $E_\phi$, $F_\beta^\omega$ in the camouflaged object detection task. It would be beneficial if the author could provide further data pertaining to these evaluation metrics to enrich the analysis.

Questions:
1.	In Figure 3, the peak cosine similarity of the generated adversarial perturbation is observed between the 20th and 30th iteration. So will increasing the iterative step of generating adversarial perturbation enhance the transferability? 
2.	Model ensemble is an effective method to enhance the adversarial attacks’ transferability. Will the ensemble of different SAMs benefit the UMI-GRAT?

Limitations:
The experimental results are all based on SAMs and their downstream models. It will provide more valuable insights if expanding the scope of analysis to assess whether this adversarial threat also applies to other large foundation models.

Rating:
8
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper tackles an important security concern arising from deploying models fine-tuned from large foundation models in private downstream settings. It introduces UMI-GRAT (Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient-Robust Adversarial Attack), designed to compromise SAM and a range of its downstream variants without relying on any prior knowledge of the downstream task or data distribution. The authors analyze the difficulties of transfer-based attacks in this no-prior setting and offer theoretical insight into the update deviation that occurs when using an open-source model as a surrogate for crafting perturbations. The method is extensively evaluated on five datasets across three downstream tasks—medical image segmentation, shadow segmentation, and camouflaged object segmentation—showing strong effectiveness, transferability, and efficiency.

Strengths:
1. The paper addresses a pressing adversarial risk in deploying large foundation models and, to the best of my knowledge, is the first to study the more realistic and challenging case of attacking SAM and its downstream models without any task- or data-specific prior.
2. It provides a thorough analysis of the obstacles for transfer-based attacks when leveraging open-source SAM and contributes corresponding theoretical insights together with a practical solution.
3. The experimental setup is comprehensive, and UMI-GRAT consistently outperforms prior approaches in misleading multiple SAM-based downstream models, offering a solid first step for subsequent work.

Weaknesses:
1. A more thorough examination of the UMI noise is needed, including details about the size of the natural image corpus used and sensitivity to key hyperparameters.
2. For camouflaged object detection, additional metrics such as E_phi and F_beta^omega are standard; including these would strengthen and deepen the empirical analysis.

Questions:
1. Figure 3 shows the cosine similarity of the generated perturbation peaking between iterations 20 and 30. Would increasing the number of iterations further improve transferability?
2. Model ensembling is known to boost transferability. Would ensembling different SAM variants enhance UMI-GRAT’s performance?

Limitations:
All experiments are conducted on SAM and its downstream models. Broadening the study to other large foundation models would provide more general insights into the scope of this threat.

Rating:
8

Summary:

The author explores the challenges associated with threating transfer-based adversarial attack without the task-related prior knowledge and provides the theoretical insights on the deviation in updating the adversarial perturbation when using the open-sourced model as the surrogate model. An extensive evaluation of UMI-GRAT's performance, transferability, and efficiency was conducted across five datasets and three different downstream tasks (medical image segmentation, shadow segmentation and camouflaged object segmentation), demonstrating the high effectiveness of the UMI-GRAT approach.

Strengths:
1. This work discusses a critical adversarial issue of deploying large foundation model in real-world applications and for the first time considers a more challenging and practical scenario where the adversarial attacker breaks SAM and its downstream models in the absence of prior knowledge on the task and data distribution.
2. This work provides the in-depth analysis on the challenge of threating the transferable adversarial attack via open-sourced SAM and proposes the corresponding theoretical insights and solution.  
3. The work establishes a detailed experimental framework and the proposed UMI-GRAT shows superior performance on misleading various SAMs’ downstream models compared with previous methods, which serve as a preliminary exploration for future research.

Weaknesses:
1.	It’s recommended to give more comprehensive analysis of the UMI noise, including the size of the natural image dataset and the effect of various hyperparameters.
2.	There are more metrics such as $E_\phi$, $F_\beta^\omega$ in the camouflaged object detection task. It would be beneficial if the author could provide further data pertaining to these evaluation metrics to enrich the analysis.

Questions:
1.	In Figure 3, the peak cosine similarity of the generated adversarial perturbation is observed between the 20th and 30th iteration. So will increasing the iterative step of generating adversarial perturbation enhance the transferability? 
2.	Model ensemble is an effective method to enhance the adversarial attacks’ transferability. Will the ensemble of different SAMs benefit the UMI-GRAT?

Limitations:
The experimental results are all based on SAMs and their downstream models. It will provide more valuable insights if expanding the scope of analysis to assess whether this adversarial threat also applies to other large foundation models.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors present a new approach for adversarial attacks on Segment Anything Model (SAM)-based downstream models, addressing the challenge of attacking without prior knowledge of the downstream task or data distribution. Their key contribution is a universal meta initialization-based algorithm that exposes inherent vulnerabilities in the foundation model. The authors also introduce a gradient robust loss, which simulates uncertainty through gradient-based noise augmentation. This loss is derived from a theoretical formulation of adversarial update deviation between the open-sourced SAM and its fine-tuned downstream models. The authors provide an analytical demonstration of how their proposed method enhances attack transferability. The effectiveness of their approach is thoroughly validated through comprehensive experiments.
reviews.strengths:
Originality: This is the first work to explore the feasibility of adversarially attacking various downstream models fine-tuned from the Segment Anything Model (SAM). The introduction of a universal meta initialization-based algorithm to uncover intrinsic vulnerabilities in foundation models is both effective and efficient. Additionally, the formulation of adversarial update deviation and the proposal of a gradient robust loss that simulates uncertainty with gradient-based noise augmentation further enhance the transferability of adversarial examples.

Quality and Clarity: The writing is generally clear but has room for improvement. The methodology and results are well-structured, though some technical sections could benefit from additional clarification.

Significance: This work is highly significant given the increasing prevalence of foundation models like SAM. The proposed methods for enhancing attack transferability have important implications for AI system security and could influence future directions in both offensive and defensive strategies in adversarial machine learning for SAM.
reviews.weaknesses:
1 - My major concern is related to the novelty of the proposed approach. Although I agree that this is the first work in the context of SAMs, the main components, such as downstream agnostic adversarial examples and meta learning-based fast initialization, have already been proposed in the literature.

2 - The authors, in line 45, briefly highlight downstream agnostic examples in just one line. They should clarify in the related work section how their work is different from references 55 and 56 of the main paper, beyond just applying it to SAM. Similarly, another related work that the authors missed is [1] (given below), in which the generated adversarial examples are agnostic to downstream tasks.

3 - Similarly, the authors did not mention any work related to meta-learning-based adversarial examples in the paper. There are multiple works that use meta-learning to craft universal adversarial examples, such as [1, 2] below. The authors use these meta-learning-based methods for initialization of adversarial examples, but this has already been explored in [3] below. The authors should mention these meta-learning-based approaches in their paper and discuss how their method is different from these approaches, beyond just the application to SAMs.

4 - It is not clear to me when the authors claim in line 8 that they are attacking "without accessing the downstream task." What is the task here? Is it not the segmentation task? In [1], their task-agnostic adversarial examples are effective against classification, detection, and segmentation. Since the downstream task here is segmentation-based, is it not obvious what the task is? Please clarify this.

5 - The authors should include some specific aspects of SAM to make their attack more unique. Currently, they are utilizing the SAM image encoder, which, in my opinion, is not much different from the previous works listed below.

6 - For experiments, why have the authors compared their method with intermediate-level feature-based approaches? They should also compare it with different downstream agnostic adversarial approaches as listed below.

7 - In Equation 8, how did the authors choose the threshold lambda? 

[1] A Self-supervised Approach for Adversarial Robustness (CVPR-2020)

[2] Learning to Generate Image Source-Agnostic Universal Adversarial Perturbations (IJCAI22)

[3] Meta Adversarial Perturbations (AAI2022-Workshop)

[4] Adversarial Initialization with Universal Adversarial Perturbation: A New Approach to Fast Adversarial Training
reviews.questions: Please see the weakness section. While the paper presents an approach to attacking SAM-based downstream models, it largely combines existing methods rather than introducing new techniques. The current strategy, though effective, does not fully exploit SAM's unique architecture.
reviews.limitations: Yes.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors present a new approach for adversarial attacks on Segment Anything Model (SAM)-based downstream models, addressing the challenge of attacking without prior knowledge of the downstream task or data distribution. Their key contribution is a universal meta initialization-based algorithm that exposes inherent vulnerabilities in the foundation model. The authors also introduce a gradient robust loss, which simulates uncertainty through gradient-based noise augmentation. This loss is derived from a theoretical formulation of adversarial update deviation between the open-sourced SAM and its fine-tuned downstream models. The authors provide an analytical demonstration of how their proposed method enhances attack transferability. The effectiveness of their approach is thoroughly validated through comprehensive experiments.

Strengths:
Originality: This is the first work to explore the feasibility of adversarially attacking various downstream models fine-tuned from the Segment Anything Model (SAM). The introduction of a universal meta initialization-based algorithm to uncover intrinsic vulnerabilities in foundation models is both effective and efficient. Additionally, the formulation of adversarial update deviation and the proposal of a gradient robust loss that simulates uncertainty with gradient-based noise augmentation further enhance the transferability of adversarial examples.

Quality and Clarity: The writing is generally clear but has room for improvement. The methodology and results are well-structured, though some technical sections could benefit from additional clarification.

Significance: This work is highly significant given the increasing prevalence of foundation models like SAM. The proposed methods for enhancing attack transferability have important implications for AI system security and could influence future directions in both offensive and defensive strategies in adversarial machine learning for SAM.

Weaknesses:
1 - My major concern is related to the novelty of the proposed approach. Although I agree that this is the first work in the context of SAMs, the main components, such as downstream agnostic adversarial examples and meta learning-based fast initialization, have already been proposed in the literature.

2 - The authors, in line 45, briefly highlight downstream agnostic examples in just one line. They should clarify in the related work section how their work is different from references 55 and 56 of the main paper, beyond just applying it to SAM. Similarly, another related work that the authors missed is [1] (given below), in which the generated adversarial examples are agnostic to downstream tasks.

3 - Similarly, the authors did not mention any work related to meta-learning-based adversarial examples in the paper. There are multiple works that use meta-learning to craft universal adversarial examples, such as [1, 2] below. The authors use these meta-learning-based methods for initialization of adversarial examples, but this has already been explored in [3] below. The authors should mention these meta-learning-based approaches in their paper and discuss how their method is different from these approaches, beyond just the application to SAMs.

4 - It is not clear to me when the authors claim in line 8 that they are attacking "without accessing the downstream task." What is the task here? Is it not the segmentation task? In [1], their task-agnostic adversarial examples are effective against classification, detection, and segmentation. Since the downstream task here is segmentation-based, is it not obvious what the task is? Please clarify this.

5 - The authors should include some specific aspects of SAM to make their attack more unique. Currently, they are utilizing the SAM image encoder, which, in my opinion, is not much different from the previous works listed below.

6 - For experiments, why have the authors compared their method with intermediate-level feature-based approaches? They should also compare it with different downstream agnostic adversarial approaches as listed below.

7 - In Equation 8, how did the authors choose the threshold lambda? 

[1] A Self-supervised Approach for Adversarial Robustness (CVPR-2020)

[2] Learning to Generate Image Source-Agnostic Universal Adversarial Perturbations (IJCAI22)

[3] Meta Adversarial Perturbations (AAI2022-Workshop)

[4] Adversarial Initialization with Universal Adversarial Perturbation: A New Approach to Fast Adversarial Training

Questions:
Please see the weakness section. While the paper presents an approach to attacking SAM-based downstream models, it largely combines existing methods rather than introducing new techniques. The current strategy, though effective, does not fully exploit SAM's unique architecture.

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
4
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new adversarial attack strategy targeting models built on top of the Segment Anything Model (SAM), aiming to operate without prior knowledge of the specific downstream task or data distribution. The central idea is a universal meta-initialization algorithm designed to reveal intrinsic weaknesses in the SAM foundation model. The authors further introduce a gradient-robust loss that injects gradient-based noise to mimic uncertainty. This loss is grounded in a theoretical analysis of adversarial update deviation between the publicly released SAM and its fine-tuned variants. The paper provides an analytical argument for why the method improves transferability, and supports the claims with extensive experimental evidence.

Strengths:
- Originality: To my knowledge, this is the first paper to systematically study adversarial attacks against a range of SAM-derived downstream models. The universal meta initialization is both efficient and effective at surfacing vulnerabilities in a foundation model. Additionally, the adversarial update deviation formulation and the proposed gradient-robust loss—implemented via gradient-driven noise augmentation—contribute to improved transferability of adversarial examples.
- Quality and clarity: The paper is generally well organized and readable, though certain technical passages would benefit from clearer exposition.
- Significance: Given the growing importance of foundation models like SAM, this work is timely and relevant. Methods that enhance cross-model attack transferability have important ramifications for the security of AI systems and may shape upcoming research in both attack and defense for SAM-based pipelines.

Weaknesses:
1 - Primary novelty concern: Although this appears to be the first exploration in the SAM setting, key components—downstream-agnostic adversarial examples and meta-learning-based rapid initialization—have prior art in the literature.

2 - Related work on downstream-agnostic attacks is under-discussed. The manuscript briefly mentions such approaches (line 45) but only in passing. Please clarify in the related work section how your method differs from references 55 and 56 in the main paper beyond merely applying them to SAM. Also, the paper omits [1], which similarly produces adversarial examples that are agnostic to downstream tasks.

3 - The paper does not acknowledge prior meta-learning-based adversarial example methods. Several works have used meta-learning to craft universal adversarial perturbations ([1, 2] below), and meta-learning has already been employed for initializing adversarial examples ([3]). Please discuss these approaches and articulate how your method diverges from them beyond the SAM application focus.

4 - Clarification needed on “without accessing the downstream task” (line 8). What exactly is the task here? Is it not segmentation? In [1], task-agnostic adversarial examples transfer across classification, detection, and segmentation. Since the downstream setting here is segmentation, isn’t the task known? Please elaborate.

5 - The attack would benefit from incorporating SAM-specific characteristics to distinguish it more clearly. As it stands, the method primarily leverages the SAM image encoder, which does not feel substantially different from prior work cited below.

6 - Experimental comparisons: Why compare primarily against intermediate-level feature-based baselines? Please also include comparisons to other downstream-agnostic adversarial methods listed below.

7 - Equation 8: How is the threshold lambda selected?

[1] A Self-supervised Approach for Adversarial Robustness (CVPR-2020)
[2] Learning to Generate Image Source-Agnostic Universal Adversarial Perturbations (IJCAI22)
[3] Meta Adversarial Perturbations (AAI2022-Workshop)
[4] Adversarial Initialization with Universal Adversarial Perturbation: A New Approach to Fast Adversarial Training

Questions:
Please see the weaknesses above. While the paper presents a method tailored to SAM-based downstream models, it primarily assembles existing ideas rather than contributing fundamentally new techniques, and it does not fully leverage architectural features unique to SAM.

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
4

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose an adversarial attack method that can contaminate downstream tasks from the perspective of adversarial transferability. They address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing universal meta initialization. In this paper, the authors address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing UMI noise. The authors introduce the idea of meta-learning to allow their algorithm to quickly adapt to different situations, i.e., downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. The theoretical part of this paper is detailed, the experiments are sufficient. The comparison with other methods shows the sophistication of their approach.



2. The attacks proposed in this paper are novel. It contributes to the topic of attacking downstream tasks of large models. A discussion on adversarial transferability is introduced under this topic.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The readability of the Methodology section of this article is somewhat poor. The authors define the problem to be solved through the form of propositions. Similarly, if the authors could summarise the formulas as Theorem and put the proof process (both formulas and reasoning) specifically in the supplementary material, it would make the article more coherent.



2. The randomness of the experimental results is unknown. I understand that due to the larger computational effort, it is not practical to report error lines on all major experiments. But it would be better for the authors to report a set of randomness on a smaller dataset and simpler settings, which will influence the reviewers' opinion of the results of this method.
reviews.questions: Two questions listed, see Weaknesses for details. Note that if the authors can demonstrate the randomness of their algorithms, that will help to get a higher rating.
reviews.limitations: The authors correctly list the implications of their work for the use of large models such as SAM in downstream tasks.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose an adversarial attack method that can contaminate downstream tasks from the perspective of adversarial transferability. They address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing universal meta initialization. In this paper, the authors address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing UMI noise. The authors introduce the idea of meta-learning to allow their algorithm to quickly adapt to different situations, i.e., downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. The theoretical part of this paper is detailed, the experiments are sufficient. The comparison with other methods shows the sophistication of their approach.



2. The attacks proposed in this paper are novel. It contributes to the topic of attacking downstream tasks of large models. A discussion on adversarial transferability is introduced under this topic.

Weaknesses:
1. The readability of the Methodology section of this article is somewhat poor. The authors define the problem to be solved through the form of propositions. Similarly, if the authors could summarise the formulas as Theorem and put the proof process (both formulas and reasoning) specifically in the supplementary material, it would make the article more coherent.



2. The randomness of the experimental results is unknown. I understand that due to the larger computational effort, it is not practical to report error lines on all major experiments. But it would be better for the authors to report a set of randomness on a smaller dataset and simpler settings, which will influence the reviewers' opinion of the results of this method.

Questions:
Two questions listed, see Weaknesses for details. Note that if the authors can demonstrate the randomness of their algorithms, that will help to get a higher rating.

Limitations:
The authors correctly list the implications of their work for the use of large models such as SAM in downstream tasks.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an adversarial attack framework aimed at contaminating downstream tasks through adversarial transferability. The key idea is to mitigate the divergence in optimization trajectories that arises when SAM models are fine-tuned for different downstream tasks. To this end, the authors design a universal meta initialization (UMI), also referred to as UMI noise, and incorporate meta-learning so the attack can adapt rapidly across varied downstream settings.

Strengths:
- The theoretical development is thorough, and the experimental evaluation is extensive. Comparisons against existing baselines indicate the sophistication and competitiveness of the proposed approach.
- The attack strategy is original and pushes forward the study of attacking downstream tasks of large models. The work also brings an explicit discussion of adversarial transferability in this context.

Weaknesses:
- The Methodology section is difficult to follow. The problem is framed via propositions, which hampers readability. It would improve coherence to consolidate the main formulas into a Theorem and move the proofs (including derivations and reasoning) to the supplementary material.
- The extent of randomness in the results is unclear. While it is understandable that reporting error bars for all major experiments may be impractical due to computational cost, the authors should at least provide variability analysis on a smaller dataset with simpler settings. This would meaningfully affect reviewers’ confidence in the results.

Questions:
- Two questions, both tied to the weaknesses above: (1) Can the authors restructure the methodology as suggested (Theorem in the main text, proofs in the supplement)? (2) Can the authors quantify the randomness of their algorithm? Demonstrating the latter would strengthen the paper and could lead to a higher rating.

Limitations:
The paper appropriately acknowledges the broader implications for deploying large models like SAM in downstream tasks.

Rating:
5

reviews.summary: This paper investigates the vulnerability of Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its downstream models to transferable adversarial attacks. The authors propose a novel attack method called Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient Robust Adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) that leverages the open-sourced SAM to generate adversarial examples effective against fine-tuned downstream models, even without access to the downstream task or dataset.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper tackles a practical and challenging problem of attacking downstream models fine-tuned from a publicly available foundation model without knowledge of the downstream task or data.
2. The proposed UMI-GRAT method is well-motivated and technically sound. The authors provide theoretical insights into the gradient deviation problem and propose a robust solution using gradient noise augmentation.
3. The paper presents extensive experiments demonstrating the effectiveness of UMI-GRAT in attacking SAM and its downstream models
reviews.weaknesses: See Questions.
reviews.questions:
1. How does the performance of UMI-GRAT vary with different choices of hyperparameters, such as the perturbation bound ε and the number of iterations in UMI and LGR. Especially, line 278 mentions that the perturbation bound is 10, which is a bit too large.
2. According to the latest benchmark **[R1]**, baselines used in the paper are not SOTA methods. How does it compare with NCS **[R2]**, ANDA **[R3]**, DeCowA **[R4]** and L2T **[R5]**?
3. Is the proposed method a universal transfer attack method? Although this question is mentioned on line 548, can the performance of UMI-GRAT and SOTA be compared under a general transfer attack test setting?
4. UMI-GRAT is a gradient-based attack method. How does the proposed method perform when the model has a certain robustness (such as adversarial training)?

---

**[R2]** Enhancing Adversarial Transferability Through Neighborhood Conditional Sampling.   
**[R3]** Strong Transferable Adversarial Attacks via Ensembled Asymptotically Normal Distribution Learning. CVPR. 2024.   
**[R4]** Boosting Adversarial Transferability across Model Genus by Deformation-Constrained Warping.   
**[R5]** Learning to Transform Dynamically for Better Adversarial Transferability

Summary:
This paper investigates the vulnerability of Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its downstream models to transferable adversarial attacks. The authors propose a novel attack method called Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient Robust Adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) that leverages the open-sourced SAM to generate adversarial examples effective against fine-tuned downstream models, even without access to the downstream task or dataset.

Strengths:
1. The paper tackles a practical and challenging problem of attacking downstream models fine-tuned from a publicly available foundation model without knowledge of the downstream task or data.
2. The proposed UMI-GRAT method is well-motivated and technically sound. The authors provide theoretical insights into the gradient deviation problem and propose a robust solution using gradient noise augmentation.
3. The paper presents extensive experiments demonstrating the effectiveness of UMI-GRAT in attacking SAM and its downstream models

Weaknesses:
See Questions.

Questions:
1. How does the performance of UMI-GRAT vary with different choices of hyperparameters, such as the perturbation bound ε and the number of iterations in UMI and LGR. Especially, line 278 mentions that the perturbation bound is 10, which is a bit too large.

4. UMI-GRAT is a gradient-based attack method. How does the proposed method perform when the model has a certain robustness (such as adversarial training)?

---

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper studies how susceptible the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and models fine-tuned from it are to transferable adversarial attacks. It introduces a new attack, Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient Robust Adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT), which exploits the publicly available SAM to craft adversarial examples that transfer to downstream fine-tuned models, even without any access to the downstream task or data.

Strengths:
1. The work addresses a realistic and challenging scenario: attacking downstream models derived from a public foundation model without knowledge of the target task or dataset.
2. UMI-GRAT is well-justified and methodologically solid. The paper offers theoretical analysis on gradient deviation and proposes a robust remedy via gradient noise augmentation.
3. The experimental evaluation is extensive and shows that UMI-GRAT effectively compromises SAM and its fine-tuned descendants.

Weaknesses:
See Questions.

Questions:
1. How sensitive is UMI-GRAT to hyperparameters such as the perturbation budget ε and the iteration counts in UMI and LGR? In particular, line 278 states a perturbation bound of 10, which seems rather large.
2. Per the latest benchmark [R1], the baselines in this paper are not state-of-the-art. How does UMI-GRAT compare against NCS [R2], ANDA [R3], DeCowA [R4], and L2T [R5]?
3. Is UMI-GRAT intended as a universal transfer attack? Although this is briefly mentioned at line 548, can you report results comparing UMI-GRAT and SOTA methods under a standard transfer attack evaluation protocol?
4. Since UMI-GRAT is gradient-based, how does it fare against models with some built-in robustness (e.g., adversarially trained models)?

References:
- [R1] Devling into Adversarial Transferability on Image Classification: A Review, Benchmark and Evaluation.

- [R5] Learning to Transform Dynamically for Better Adversarial Transferability.

Limitations:
The limitations are addressed in the Appendix.

title: Follow Hamiltonian Leader: An Efficient Energy-Guided Sampling Method

reviews.summary:
This paper presents an interesting new approach for improving sampling methods for energy-based generative models and score-matching models. The key idea is to incorporate zeroth-order information (energy values) in addition to the typical first-order gradient information used by most sampling algorithms like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC).

The authors identify several challenging scenarios where relying solely on gradients can be problematic - cases of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability. They argue that incorporating energy values can help mitigate issues in these situations and improve sampling efficiency and quality.

Overall, the core idea of leveraging zeroth-order information in addition to gradients is quite novel and the FHL algorithm is an elegant way to implement this for improving sampling efficiency and quality. The paper is well-motivated, the method is clearly explained, and the empirical results are compelling.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel idea of incorporating zeroth-order energy information into sampling algorithms like HMC, which typically only use gradients. This can help address issues like instability and metastability.

2. The Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) algorithm is an elegant way to exchange both energy and gradient information across parallel sampling chains in a principled manner.

3. Thorough experimental evaluation across synthetic examples illustrating the identified challenging scenarios of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

4. Promising results showing improved sampling quality over baselines for energy-based generative models on real datasets like CLEVR.

5. Clear motivation and well-explained methodology.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It would be better to show exploration of the sensitivity to key hyperparameters like the number of parallel sampling chains.

2. Discussion of computational cost/overhead compared to baseline sampling methods are missing in the manuscript.
reviews.questions:
1. While the empirical results are compelling, can you provide any theoretical analysis or guarantees about the convergence properties, sampling quality, or stationary distribution of the FHL algorithm? Even approximate bounds or insights would strengthen the theoretical grounding.

2. What are the main computational and memory overheads introduced by running parallel chains in FHL compared to standard HMC? Is there a limit on scalability to high dimensions or is there a way to reduce overhead through approximations?
reviews.limitations: Based on the provided paper, the authors do not appear to have explicitly discussed the limitations or potential negative societal impacts of their work. The paper is primarily focused on presenting the technical details of the proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) sampling algorithm and its empirical evaluation.

Summary:
This paper presents an interesting new approach for improving sampling methods for energy-based generative models and score-matching models. The key idea is to incorporate zeroth-order information (energy values) in addition to the typical first-order gradient information used by most sampling algorithms like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC).

The authors identify several challenging scenarios where relying solely on gradients can be problematic - cases of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability. They argue that incorporating energy values can help mitigate issues in these situations and improve sampling efficiency and quality.

Overall, the core idea of leveraging zeroth-order information in addition to gradients is quite novel and the FHL algorithm is an elegant way to implement this for improving sampling efficiency and quality. The paper is well-motivated, the method is clearly explained, and the empirical results are compelling.

Strengths:
1. Novel idea of incorporating zeroth-order energy information into sampling algorithms like HMC, which typically only use gradients. This can help address issues like instability and metastability.

2. The Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) algorithm is an elegant way to exchange both energy and gradient information across parallel sampling chains in a principled manner.

3. Thorough experimental evaluation across synthetic examples illustrating the identified challenging scenarios of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

4. Promising results showing improved sampling quality over baselines for energy-based generative models on real datasets like CLEVR.

5. Clear motivation and well-explained methodology.

Weaknesses:
1. It would be better to show exploration of the sensitivity to key hyperparameters like the number of parallel sampling chains.

2. Discussion of computational cost/overhead compared to baseline sampling methods are missing in the manuscript.

Questions:
1. While the empirical results are compelling, can you provide any theoretical analysis or guarantees about the convergence properties, sampling quality, or stationary distribution of the FHL algorithm? Even approximate bounds or insights would strengthen the theoretical grounding.

2. What are the main computational and memory overheads introduced by running parallel chains in FHL compared to standard HMC? Is there a limit on scalability to high dimensions or is there a way to reduce overhead through approximations?

Limitations:
Based on the provided paper, the authors do not appear to have explicitly discussed the limitations or potential negative societal impacts of their work. The paper is primarily focused on presenting the technical details of the proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) sampling algorithm and its empirical evaluation.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work proposes a fresh approach to improving samplers for energy-based generative models and score-matching methods by augmenting the usual gradient information with zeroth-order signals, namely the energy values themselves. The authors highlight several regimes—instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability—where gradient-only schemes such as Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) can struggle, and argue that incorporating energies can alleviate these issues and enhance both efficiency and sample quality. The Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) algorithm operationalizes this idea by enabling principled exchange of both energy and gradient information across multiple parallel chains. The paper is well-motivated, the technique is laid out clearly, and the empirical evidence is persuasive.

Strengths:
1. Introduces the novel use of zeroth-order energy information within samplers that typically rely exclusively on gradients, offering a way to handle problematic regimes like instability and metastability.
2. The FHL method provides a neat, principled mechanism for sharing energy and gradient signals across parallel chains.
3. Comprehensive experiments on synthetic tasks that directly target the identified challenging regimes (instability, metastability, pseudo-stability).
4. Encouraging gains in sampling quality on real data for energy-based generative models, including results on CLEVR.
5. Strong motivation and a clear presentation of the methodology.

Weaknesses:
1. Sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters—especially the number of parallel chains—is not explored.
2. The manuscript lacks a discussion of computational overhead relative to baseline samplers.

Questions:
1. Beyond the empirical results, can the authors provide theoretical analysis or guarantees regarding convergence behavior, sample quality, or the stationary distribution of FHL? Even partial bounds or heuristic insights would strengthen the theory.
2. What are the dominant computational and memory costs of running parallel chains in FHL compared to standard HMC? Are there scalability limits in high dimensions, and can approximations reduce the overhead?

Limitations:
The paper does not explicitly address limitations or potential negative societal impacts. The focus is primarily on the technical development of FHL and its empirical performance.

reviews.summary: This work proposes to incorporate the energy $U$ into the gradient-based sampling techniques. In particular, it proposes to choose the lowest energy particle as the leader and then add an extra elastic tension between the leader and followers in the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo method.
reviews.strengths: The idea is simple and clear, the toy examples are easy to understand and demonstrate the benefit of the proposed method well. In addition, the authors conduct experiments for each of the three challenging sampling scenarios identified by the authors.
reviews.weaknesses:
It might worth including the overhead of the proposed method, how much slower the algorithm is per iteration compared to HMC for instance.

The tension coefficient $\lambda$ is critical, setting it to 0 recovers the baseline. But I did not find an ablation over the $\lambda$, is it hard to choose? From my understanding, if you set $\lambda$ pretty large it might recover something like gradient descent and the sampling will collapse.
reviews.questions:
1. How is $\lambda$ determined per experiment? Is it possible to include an ablation over it for the three scenarios at least?

2. In Figure 7, it is unclear to me which method is better. It will be better to provide some quantitative measurement like FID or IS scores?
reviews.limitations: Yes.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work proposes to incorporate the energy $U$ into the gradient-based sampling techniques. In particular, it proposes to choose the lowest energy particle as the leader and then add an extra elastic tension between the leader and followers in the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo method.

Strengths:
The idea is simple and clear, the toy examples are easy to understand and demonstrate the benefit of the proposed method well. In addition, the authors conduct experiments for each of the three challenging sampling scenarios identified by the authors.

Weaknesses:
It might worth including the overhead of the proposed method, how much slower the algorithm is per iteration compared to HMC for instance.

The tension coefficient $\lambda$ is critical, setting it to 0 recovers the baseline. But I did not find an ablation over the $\lambda$, is it hard to choose? From my understanding, if you set $\lambda$ pretty large it might recover something like gradient descent and the sampling will collapse.

Questions:
1. How is $\lambda$ determined per experiment? Is it possible to include an ablation over it for the three scenarios at least?

2. In Figure 7, it is unclear to me which method is better. It will be better to provide some quantitative measurement like FID or IS scores?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper augments gradient-based samplers by explicitly leveraging the potential energy U. Concretely, it designates the particle with the lowest energy as a leader and introduces an additional elastic coupling between this leader and the followers within an HMC framework.

Strengths:
The core idea is straightforward and well-articulated. The toy experiments are intuitive and effectively showcase the benefits of the approach. Moreover, the authors run studies across each of the three difficult sampling settings they identify.

Weaknesses:
It would be helpful to report the computational overhead of the proposed modification—e.g., how much slower each iteration is compared to standard HMC. The tension parameter λ is pivotal; λ = 0 reduces to the baseline, yet there is no ablation on λ. Is it challenging to tune? Based on my understanding, setting λ too high risks turning the dynamics into something akin to gradient descent, potentially causing the sampler to collapse.

Questions:
1. How is λ selected in each experiment? Could you add an ablation over λ, at least for the three presented scenarios?
2. In Figure 7, it’s not clear which method is superior. Could you include quantitative metrics such as FID or IS to clarify?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6

reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel parallel sampling method named "Follow Hamiltonian Leader" (FHL) designed to address sampling challenges by leveraging zeroth-order information, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the sampling process. Experimental results indicate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and outperforms traditional sampling techniques, especially in scenarios involving corrupted gradients.
reviews.strengths:
1. Innovative combination of zeroth and first-order information.
2. The effectiveness of the method is demonstrated in multiple task scenarios.
3. Theoretical analysis and prove are sufficient.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Is there any quantitative experiments like evaluating FID and IS on cifar10 datasets and I think it's more compelling whether a novel sampling methods combined with generative models can be used on image datasets with more complex distributions.
2. Lack of experiment of OOD in combination with EBMs or score-based models to valid the stability during sampling with proposed method.
reviews.questions:
Why is E(x, \tilde x) defined in this way. And is there any relation between extra elastic energy and local entropy [1].


[1] P. Chaudhari, A. Choromanska, S. Soatto, Y. LeCun, C. Baldassi, C. Borgs, J. T. Chayes, L. Sagun, and R. Zecchina. Entropy-SGD: Biasing gradient descent into wide valleys. In ICLR, 2017.
reviews.limitations:
1. Limited exploration of integration with other advanced MCMC methods.
2. Lack of quantitative experiments to demonstrate the advantage of proposed sampling method compared with other methods.

Summary:
This paper presents a novel parallel sampling method named "Follow Hamiltonian Leader" (FHL) designed to address sampling challenges by leveraging zeroth-order information, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the sampling process. Experimental results indicate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and outperforms traditional sampling techniques, especially in scenarios involving corrupted gradients.

Strengths:
1. Innovative combination of zeroth and first-order information.
2. The effectiveness of the method is demonstrated in multiple task scenarios.
3. Theoretical analysis and prove are sufficient.

Weaknesses:
1. Is there any quantitative experiments like evaluating FID and IS on cifar10 datasets and I think it's more compelling whether a novel sampling methods combined with generative models can be used on image datasets with more complex distributions.
2. Lack of experiment of OOD in combination with EBMs or score-based models to valid the stability during sampling with proposed method.

Questions:
Why is E(x, \tilde x) defined in this way. And is there any relation between extra elastic energy and local entropy [1].

Limitations:

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a parallel sampling framework, Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), which leverages zeroth-order cues to cope with situations where gradient information is noisy, unreliable, or absent. By using a leader-driven guidance mechanism, the approach aims to accelerate and improve exploration of the target distribution. Empirical results suggest that FHL markedly enhances coverage and outperforms conventional samplers, particularly under corrupted-gradient settings.

Strengths:
1. Creative and effective integration of zeroth- and first-order information.
2. Demonstrated effectiveness across several task settings.
3. Theoretical development and accompanying proofs are adequate.

Weaknesses:
1. Missing quantitative benchmarks such as FID and IS on CIFAR-10. It would be more persuasive to show that this new sampler, when combined with generative models, scales to image datasets with more complex distributions.
2. No out-of-distribution experiments with EBMs or score-based models, which are important to verify the stability of the proposed sampling procedure.

Questions:
- What is the rationale for the specific definition of E(x, \tilde x)? Is there a connection between the proposed extra elastic energy and local entropy as in Entropy-SGD [1]?

[1] P. Chaudhari, A. Choromanska, S. Soatto, Y. LeCun, C. Baldassi, C. Borgs, J. T. Chayes, L. Sagun, and R. Zecchina. Entropy-SGD: Biasing gradient descent into wide valleys. In ICLR, 2017.

Limitations:
1. Limited investigation into how the method integrates with other advanced MCMC techniques.
2. Lack of comprehensive quantitative comparisons demonstrating the advantage of the proposed sampler over alternatives.

Rating:
6

reviews.summary: This paper introduces an interesting parallel sampling method that leverages zeroth-order information to address challenges in sampling from probability distributions, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness by connecting multiple sampling instances through a selected leader. The proposed method, named Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), extends the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework by concurrently running multiple replicas at different energy levels and combining both zeroth and first-order information from various chains. Experimental results demonstrate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and produces higher-quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques, showing resilience against corrupted gradients and excelling in scenarios characterized by instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.
reviews.strengths:
- The proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) method markedly improves the efficiency and effectiveness of sampling processes, significantly expediting the exploration of target distributions and producing superior quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques.
- FHL demonstrates greater resilience against the detrimental impacts of corrupted gradients by incorporating zeroth-order information. This robustness makes the method particularly valuable in scenarios where first-order information is compromised, ensuring more reliable and accurate sampling.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The proposed FHL method involves intricate modifications to the traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo framework, such as the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic leapfrog technique, which may increase the complexity of implementation and require significant computational resources.
- The effectiveness of the FHL method heavily relies on the appropriate selection of the leader particle. If the leader is not accurately chosen, it could lead to suboptimal sampling performance, potentially compromising the overall efficiency and accuracy of the method.
- While the paper presents experimental results to demonstrate the efficacy of the FHL method, there is a lack of in-depth theoretical analysis to rigorously establish the convergence properties and performance guarantees of the proposed approach.
- The method’s scalability to high-dimensional problems or extremely large datasets is not thoroughly addressed. The parallel sampling approach may encounter challenges in maintaining efficiency and effectiveness as the dimensionality and size of the data increase.
reviews.questions: See weaknesses above.
reviews.limitations: The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces an interesting parallel sampling method that leverages zeroth-order information to address challenges in sampling from probability distributions, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness by connecting multiple sampling instances through a selected leader. The proposed method, named Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), extends the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework by concurrently running multiple replicas at different energy levels and combining both zeroth and first-order information from various chains. Experimental results demonstrate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and produces higher-quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques, showing resilience against corrupted gradients and excelling in scenarios characterized by instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

Strengths:
- The proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) method markedly improves the efficiency and effectiveness of sampling processes, significantly expediting the exploration of target distributions and producing superior quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques.
- FHL demonstrates greater resilience against the detrimental impacts of corrupted gradients by incorporating zeroth-order information. This robustness makes the method particularly valuable in scenarios where first-order information is compromised, ensuring more reliable and accurate sampling.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed FHL method involves intricate modifications to the traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo framework, such as the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic leapfrog technique, which may increase the complexity of implementation and require significant computational resources.
- The effectiveness of the FHL method heavily relies on the appropriate selection of the leader particle. If the leader is not accurately chosen, it could lead to suboptimal sampling performance, potentially compromising the overall efficiency and accuracy of the method.
- While the paper presents experimental results to demonstrate the efficacy of the FHL method, there is a lack of in-depth theoretical analysis to rigorously establish the convergence properties and performance guarantees of the proposed approach.
- The method’s scalability to high-dimensional problems or extremely large datasets is not thoroughly addressed. The parallel sampling approach may encounter challenges in maintaining efficiency and effectiveness as the dimensionality and size of the data increase.

Questions:
See weaknesses above.

Limitations:
The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations.

Rating:
4
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a parallel sampling approach that exploits zeroth-order information to tackle situations where gradients are unreliable or unavailable. The method introduces a leader-guided strategy that links multiple sampling chains via a chosen leader to improve efficiency and effectiveness. Termed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), the approach extends HMC by running multiple replicas at varying energy levels and aggregating both zeroth- and first-order information across chains. Empirical results indicate that FHL enhances exploration of target distributions and yields higher-quality samples relative to standard methods, demonstrating robustness to corrupted gradients and strong performance in unstable, metastable, and pseudo-stable regimes.

Strengths:
- FHL notably accelerates and improves sampling, delivering better exploration and higher-quality results than conventional techniques.
- By incorporating zeroth-order signals, FHL shows increased robustness to corrupted gradients, making it particularly useful when first-order information is unreliable and enabling more dependable sampling.

Weaknesses:
- FHL adds substantial complexity to the HMC framework—e.g., the leader-guiding mechanism and the elastic leapfrog scheme—which may complicate implementation and increase computational overhead.
- The method’s performance is highly contingent on selecting an appropriate leader particle; a poor choice can degrade sampling quality and undermine overall efficiency and accuracy.
- Despite supportive experiments, the paper lacks a thorough theoretical treatment establishing convergence guarantees and formal performance bounds.
- Scalability to high-dimensional settings or very large datasets is insufficiently discussed; the parallel design may struggle to sustain efficiency and effectiveness as dimensionality and data size grow.

Questions:
See weaknesses above.

Limitations:
The manuscript does not sufficiently discuss or analyze its limitations.

Rating:
4
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces an interesting parallel sampling method that leverages zeroth-order information to address challenges in sampling from probability distributions, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness by connecting multiple sampling instances through a selected leader. The proposed method, named Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), extends the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework by concurrently running multiple replicas at different energy levels and combining both zeroth and first-order information from various chains. Experimental results demonstrate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and produces higher-quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques, showing resilience against corrupted gradients and excelling in scenarios characterized by instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

Strengths:
- The proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) method markedly improves the efficiency and effectiveness of sampling processes, significantly expediting the exploration of target distributions and producing superior quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques.
- FHL demonstrates greater resilience against the detrimental impacts of corrupted gradients by incorporating zeroth-order information. This robustness makes the method particularly valuable in scenarios where first-order information is compromised, ensuring more reliable and accurate sampling.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed FHL method involves intricate modifications to the traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo framework, such as the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic leapfrog technique, which may increase the complexity of implementation and require significant computational resources.
- The effectiveness of the FHL method heavily relies on the appropriate selection of the leader particle. If the leader is not accurately chosen, it could lead to suboptimal sampling performance, potentially compromising the overall efficiency and accuracy of the method.
- While the paper presents experimental results to demonstrate the efficacy of the FHL method, there is a lack of in-depth theoretical analysis to rigorously establish the convergence properties and performance guarantees of the proposed approach.
- The method’s scalability to high-dimensional problems or extremely large datasets is not thoroughly addressed. The parallel sampling approach may encounter challenges in maintaining efficiency and effectiveness as the dimensionality and size of the data increase.

Questions:
See weaknesses above.

Limitations:
The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations.

title: Metric Distortion Under Probabilistic Voting

reviews.summary: Metric distortion is a framework to evaluate the "accuracy" of social choice rules, by considering a worst-case candidate and voter embedding in a metric space, and by assuming that reported votes are derived from distances in the metric space. So far, votes were assumed to be a deterministic function of the distances. The paper investigates the case where they are probabilistic functions of the distances, in the asymptotic limit of a large number of voters. The key finding is that this inverts the evaluation of some voting rules, in particular Copeland and Random Dictatorship, for highly noisy voting.
reviews.strengths:
The model introduced by the authors is an insightful generalization of previous work which, remarkably, provides a markedly different view on social choice rules. Given the growing importance of social choice in machine learning, as well as accounting for noisy inputs while considering embedded vector spaces, I believe that this work scores high in significance.

Additionally, the analysis is quite thorough, with matching lower/upper asymptotic bounds for Plurality upper bound for Copeland and lower/upper bounds for Random Dictatorship.

The paper is also fairly well written.
reviews.weaknesses:
My main concern is Lemma 3. The proof seems to argue that the constraints $\forall i, | b_i - w_i | \leq b_i + w_i$ (i.e. inequality constraint on $(i, W, B)$), but the optimization problem (7) has a constraint $\max_i | w_i - b_i | \leq \min_i b_i + w_i$. It is not clear to me why these constraints would be equivalent. Note that the latter implies the former set of constraints. Thus if $\mathcal E_{\alpha}'$ was defined with all voter-wise constraints, then it would be a minimum over a smaller set, and thus $opt(\mathcal E_\alpha') \geq opt(\mathcal E_\alpha)$. Since Lemma 3's proof actually says $\frac{SC(W, d)}{SC(B, d)} \leq 1 / opt(\mathcal E'_\alpha)$, using this inequality seems to imply the actual Lemma 3. Am I reading this correctly?

It is disappointing that the upper-bound for Copeland. It would be helpful if the authors can point out where the argument gets loose.
reviews.questions:
Could the authors clarify the proof of Lemma 3? I will happily increase my score given a convincing response.

Can the authors provide insight into why the Copeland upper bound is not tight?

More anecdotically, I wonder why "Metric Distortion" is restricted to the distortion of ranking-based algorithms. I feel that the concept is more general than that. Essentially, it is the worst social cost ratio of any voting rule, when executed on votes derived from distances in the metric space, right? In particular, it could be studied even for the social-cost minimizing geometric median, but given, e.g., noisy inputs or strategic votes? I believe that this is an interesting research venue, given that machine learning provides many tools to embed candidate features and voters' preferences in a vector space.
reviews.limitations: The paper stated its results very clearly and factually. I have no concerns about unaddressed limitations.

The paper is also fairly well written.

Weaknesses:

The proof seems to argue that the constraints $\forall i, | b_i - w_i | \leq b_i + w_i$ (i.e. inequality constraint on $(i, W, B)$), but the optimization problem (7) has a constraint $\max_i | w_i - b_i | \leq \min_i b_i + w_i$. It is not clear to me why these constraints would be equivalent. Note that the latter implies the former set of constraints. Thus if $\mathcal E_{\alpha}'$ was defined with all voter-wise constraints, then it would be a minimum over a smaller set, and thus $opt(\mathcal E_\alpha') \geq opt(\mathcal E_\alpha)$. Since Lemma 3's proof actually says $\frac{SC(W, d)}{SC(B, d)} \leq 1 / opt(\mathcal E'_\alpha)$, using this inequality seems to imply the actual Lemma 3. Am I reading this correctly?

Questions:

Can the authors provide insight into why the Copeland upper bound is not tight?

More anecdotically, I wonder why "Metric Distortion" is restricted to the distortion of ranking-based algorithms. I feel that the concept is more general than that. Essentially, it is the worst social cost ratio of any voting rule, when executed on votes derived from distances in the metric space, right? In particular, it could be studied even for the social-cost minimizing geometric median, but given, e.g., noisy inputs or strategic votes? Rating:
7
reviews.text:
Summary:
Metric distortion evaluates how well social choice rules approximate true social costs by considering worst-case embeddings of voters and candidates in a metric space, with votes derived from distances. Prior work assumed votes were deterministic functions of distance. This paper analyzes the setting where votes are probabilistic functions of distance, in the asymptotic regime with many voters. The main result is that, under high noise, this reverses the relative evaluation of certain rules, notably Copeland and Random Dictatorship.

Strengths:
- The proposed model is a thoughtful and meaningful extension of earlier work, and it yields a substantially different perspective on social choice rules. Given the rising relevance of social choice in machine learning and the need to handle noisy inputs in embedded vector spaces, the contribution is, in my view, highly significant.
- The technical development is quite comprehensive, including matching asymptotic lower and upper bounds for Plurality, an upper bound for Copeland, and both lower and upper bounds for Random Dictatorship.
- The paper is generally well written.

Weaknesses:
- My principal concern is with Lemma 3. The proof appears to rely on constraints of the form ∀i, |b_i − w_i| ≤ b_i + w_i (i.e., per-voter constraints on (i, W, B)), whereas optimization problem (7) imposes max_i |w_i − b_i| ≤ min_i (b_i + w_i). It is not clear why these would be equivalent. The latter clearly implies the former. If E′_α were defined using all the voter-wise constraints, then it would optimize over a smaller feasible region, so opt(E′_α) ≥ opt(E_α). Since the proof of Lemma 3 actually states SC(W, d)/SC(B, d) ≤ 1/opt(E′_α), invoking this inequality seems to yield the stated Lemma 3. Is this interpretation correct?
- The Copeland upper bound is disappointing. It would help if the authors could indicate exactly where slack is introduced in the argument.

Questions:
- Could the authors clarify the proof of Lemma 3? With a convincing answer, I would be happy to raise my score.
- Can the authors explain why the Copeland upper bound does not appear to be tight?
- On a more anecdotal note, why is “Metric Distortion” framed as applying only to ranking-based algorithms? The notion seems broader: it is the worst-case social cost ratio of any rule when run on votes induced by distances, correct? One could potentially examine this even for the social-cost-minimizing geometric median under noise or strategic behavior. This seems like a promising direction, especially given that machine learning offers many tools to embed candidate features and voter preferences in vector spaces.

Limitations:
The results are presented clearly and transparently. I do not see any unacknowledged limitations.

Rating:
7

Decision (Rating):
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of metric distortion in single-winner elections. The key assumption is that the voters' preferences are not exactly compatible with the metric space, but they rather agree with it with a certain probability. The authors propose several axioms that formalize the requirements for the probability distribution for it to make sense in the context of distortion. Then they provide upper bounds of distortion in the probabilitic setting for Plurality, Random Dictator and Copeland (in case of the first two rules, they provide also lower bounds).
reviews.strengths: This work is the first one to combine probabilistic voting with metric distortion, hence the novelty is clear. The paper is overall of good quality, the axioms proposed in their work make sense to me, and the results are sound. The research direction introduced in this paper can be continued in further follow-up papers.
reviews.weaknesses:
The paper could have been more clearly written --- for example, the formal notation should be introduced at the beginning of Section 2   (together the model) rather than in the middle of the introduction.

Besides, I think that Axiom 2 (Independence of Other Candidates) could have been better motivated. I can imagine that it was crucial to obtain the authors' results, but it seems rather natural to me that  in the real-life scenarios that motivated the research, the presence of additional candidates can impact the voter's probability of ranking one candidate over another.

Another weakness is that the authors only consider three rules, and the analysis of only two of them is complete --- many important rules (like Borda or PluralityVeto) are not considered at all. This could raise a question whether this amount of technical contribution is enough for a top conference like NeurIPS.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and there are no potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates metric distortion in single-winner elections under a probabilistic model: voters’ rankings are not perfectly metric-consistent but align with the underlying metric with some probability. The authors introduce a set of axioms that constrain the probability distribution to be meaningful for distortion analysis. They then derive distortion upper bounds in this probabilistic framework for Plurality, Random Dictator, and Copeland, and provide matching lower bounds for the first two rules.

Strengths:
The work is, to my knowledge, the first to bring together probabilistic voting with metric distortion, which makes its contribution clearly novel. The paper is generally well executed: the proposed axioms are reasonable, and the results appear correct. The line of inquiry is promising and should spur further research.

Weaknesses:
The exposition could be clearer. In particular, the formal notation should be moved to the start of Section 2 alongside the model, instead of appearing midway through the introduction.

I also find Axiom 2 (Independence of Other Candidates) insufficiently justified. While it may be essential for obtaining the presented results, it seems quite plausible in real-world contexts that the presence of additional candidates would influence the probability that a voter ranks one candidate above another, so stronger motivation is needed.

Another limitation is the scope of the analysis: only three rules are considered, and among them, only two are fully analyzed. Several important rules (e.g., Borda, PluralityVeto) are not addressed. This raises the question of whether the technical depth is adequate for a top-tier venue like NeurIPS.

Questions:
-

Limitations:
The authors appropriately discuss the paper’s limitations, and I do not see potential negative societal impacts arising from this work.

reviews.summary:
This paper considers metric distortion in probabilistic models of voting. In the metric distortion framework the voters and alternatives are embedded in a metric space, and given the ranked preferences the goal is to find an alternative with low distortion. In this setting these rankings come from a probabilistic model.

In the first part of the paper, there axioms are introduced and authors show which axiom is satisfied by which probabilisitic model. In the second half of the paper the goal is to find the distortion of Plurality and Copeland rules for a specific class of probabilistic models. The results show matching upper and lower bound of $m$ for plurality and constant upper bound for Copeland.
reviews.strengths:
Defining a model for distortion in stochastic models is a useful idea in future analysis of voting systems.
The idea of the paper is novel and it uses novel techniques in the second half.
I like the definition of the three axioms. I find them natural and easy to understand.
reviews.weaknesses:
My main concern is about the presentation. The preliminaries section is incomplete. The definition of distortion is hard to understand for a general audience and you made it harder by just putting the formula there. You have to add a description in words and give some intuition on why this definition makes sense. 

It's not clear how the probabilistic model works and how you define distortion on it until section 3 where you define it for a specific class. You have to formally define your probabilistic approach in Section 1.1 and also define distortion in this model. Not knowing the exact definition makes following the first paragraph of section 2 really hard. Before reading the rest of the paper I didn't understand why $P$ is a function of $d$ or why the preferences may not be consistent with the distances.

I think you have to add more intuition on the probabilisitic models. For instance you mention ground-truth in the definition of Mallows model but you have to explain in more details that how this model distributes the probabilities based on the distance to this ground-truth. The same explanations are needed for PQV.

My understanding is that the analysis that you provide works for any member of $G$, but currently the only members for which we have the final bound are PL and PQV. Is that true? If so is there any other interesting member of this class?
reviews.questions: See above.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper studies metric distortion under probabilistic models of voting. In the metric distortion framework, voters and candidates lie in a metric space; from observed rankings, the aim is to select an option with small distortion. Here, the rankings are generated by a probabilistic model.

The first part introduces axioms and identifies which probabilistic models satisfy which axioms. The second part analyzes the distortion of Plurality and Copeland for a particular family of probabilistic models. The key findings are matching upper and lower bounds of m for Plurality and a constant upper bound for Copeland.

Strengths:
- Proposing a distortion framework tailored to stochastic preference models is a valuable direction for future work in voting theory.
- The paper’s core idea is original, and the techniques used in the second half are also new.
- The three axioms are well-chosen: they feel natural and are easy to grasp.

Weaknesses:
- The main issue is the exposition. The preliminaries are not sufficiently complete. The definition of distortion is already challenging for a broad audience, and presenting it only as a formula without explanation makes it harder. Please add a verbal description and intuition for why the definition is appropriate.
- It is unclear how the probabilistic model operates and how distortion is defined on it until Section 3, where you specify a particular class. You should give a formal description of your probabilistic setup in Section 1.1 and define distortion within that model. Without precise definitions, the opening paragraph of Section 2 is very difficult to follow. Before proceeding, I could not see why P is a function of d or why the rankings might conflict with the underlying distances.
- The probabilistic models need more intuitive explanation. For example, when introducing the Mallows model you refer to a ground truth, but you should elaborate on how probabilities vary with distance from this ground truth. A similar level of explanation is needed for PQV.
- My understanding is that your analysis applies to any member of G, yet the final bounds are established only for PL and PQV. Is that correct? If so, are there other noteworthy members of this class?

Questions:
See above.

Limitations:
-

Rating:
4

Original Decision (Rating)
4
